title: The Kakeya conjecture, after Wang and Zahl

conjecture:1.1.summary: The Kakeya conjecture: for a set $\mathbb{T}$ of $\sim \delta^{-(n-1)}$ $\delta$-tubes in $\mathbb{R}^n$ in $\delta$-separated directions, $|U(\mathbb{T})| \ge c(n,\epsilon)\delta^\epsilon$ — Kakeya sets have full dimension. The target.

thm:1.2.summary: Wang–Zahl (resolution in $\mathbb{R}^3$): if $\mathbb{T}$ is a set of $\delta$-tubes in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with $\Delta_{\max}(\mathbb{T}) \lessapprox 1$, then $\mu(\mathbb{T}) \lessapprox 1$ — the Kakeya set conjecture in three dimensions.

thm:6.1.summary: Sticky Kakeya theorem: $\beta_{\mathrm{sticky}} = 0$, i.e. every sticky Kakeya set $\mathbb{T}$ has $\mu(\mathbb{T}) \lessapprox 1$. The hard case of the conjecture (the self-similar/sticky configurations).

lem:6.2.summary: Submultiplicativity of the volume defect across scales: $\mu(\mathbb{T}) \lessapprox \mu(\mathbb{T}_\rho)\,\mu(\mathbb{T}[T_\rho])$ — the multiscale factorization that makes induction on scales possible.

lem:6.6.summary: Grain structure: for a worst-case sticky Kakeya set with $\beta_{\mathrm{sticky}} > 0$, in a typical ball $B_{\sqrt{\delta}}$ the union $U(\mathbb{T}) \cap B$ is organized into slabs ('grains'), forcing a rigid fractal structure on $A$.

lem:7.1.summary: Complex conjugation structure: for a worst-case sticky Kakeya set, the grain data satisfies $A + s(x)y + \tilde{s}(y)x \approx A$ for $x \in [0,\delta^{1/4}]$, $y \in [0,\delta^{1/2}]$ — an approximate additive symmetry of the set $A$.

thm:8.1.summary: Bourgain's discretized sum-product theorem: a $\delta$-set $A \subset [0,1]$ with $|A|_\delta \approx \delta^{-s}$ and non-concentration has $|A+A|_\delta + |A \cdot A|_\delta \gtrapprox \delta^{-s-\epsilon}$ — the rigidity input that the additive symmetry contradicts.

lem:8.2.summary: Finite-field expansion dichotomy: for $A \subset \mathbb{F}_p$, either $(A-A)/(A-A) = \mathbb{F}_p$, or $|((A\cdot A)^{\oplus 3} - (A\cdot A)^{\oplus 3})/(A-A)| \ge |A|^2$ — the combinatorial core behind the sum-product growth.

lem:9.1.summary: Sum-product at many scales: if $\beta_{\mathrm{sticky}}(\delta_1) \ge \beta$ then at a much finer scale $\delta_2 \le \delta_1^K$, $\beta_{\mathrm{sticky}}(\delta_2) < \beta_{\mathrm{sticky}}(\delta_1) - \epsilon$ — the strict multiscale decrement that drives $\beta_{\mathrm{sticky}}$ to $0$.

lem:12.1.summary: Non-sticky bound: a worst-case Kakeya set that is not sticky, with $\Delta_{\max}(\mathbb{T}_B) \lessapprox 1$, satisfies $\mu(\mathbb{T}) \ll |\mathbb{T}|^\beta$ — non-stickiness already forces a gain.

def:12.1.summary: Frostman condition: a set of tubes $\tilde{\mathbb{T}}$ in a convex $K$ is Frostman if $\Delta(\tilde{\mathbb{T}}, K') \lessapprox \Delta(\tilde{\mathbb{T}}, K)$ for all convex $K' \subset K$ — uniform non-concentration at all scales.

lem:12.2.summary: High density lemma: for $\tilde{\mathbb{T}}$ Frostman in $B_1$, $|U(\tilde{\mathbb{T}})| \gtrapprox (\delta^2|\tilde{\mathbb{T}}|)^{1-\beta}\delta^{2\beta}$ — the estimate that handles the non-sticky regime and reduces it to the sticky case.

lem:14.1.summary: Frostman-to-sticky reduction: if the nested tube families are Frostman at each scale, then $\mathbb{T}$ contains a sticky Kakeya subset $\mathbb{T}'$ (and decomposes into sticky pieces) — the bridge making the sticky theorem applicable in general.

lem:14.3.summary: Good-scale selection: for a worst-case high-density example with $|\mathbb{T}| \gg \delta^{-2}$, there is a scale $a$ with $\delta \ll a \ll 1$ at which both $\mathbb{T}[T_a]$ and $\mathbb{T}_a$ are Frostman — the multiscale decomposition feeding the reduction.

edges.note: The Kakeya theorem reduces (via the high density lemma) to the sticky case, settled by the sticky Kakeya theorem.
The non-sticky regime is handled by the high density lemma, which also drives the reduction to the sticky case.
Worst-case sticky sets are forced into grain structure, the starting point of the contradiction.
$\beta_{\mathrm{sticky}} = 0$ follows from the strict multiscale decrement, iterated to drive the exponent to zero.
Grain structure is read off the multiscale factorization of the volume defect.
The approximate additive symmetry of $A$ is extracted from the grain (slab) structure.
The decrement argument feeds on the complex-conjugation/additive-symmetry structure of $A$.
The strict gain at finer scales comes from Bourgain's sum-product theorem applied to $A$, whose additive symmetry contradicts sum-product expansion.
The discretized sum-product theorem rests on the finite-field expansion dichotomy.
The high density estimate builds on the non-sticky bound.
The reduction uses the existence of a good scale for multiscale decomposition.
The non-sticky bound is stated for Frostman families.
Good-scale selection feeds the Frostman-to-sticky reduction.

main_results.description: The Kakeya conjecture and Wang–Zahl's $\mathbb{R}^3$ resolution.

sticky_case.description: The hard self-similar case: sticky Kakeya sets forced into grain structure.

contradiction_via_sumproduct.description: Grain structure yields an additive symmetry that contradicts Bourgain's sum-product theorem across scales.

nonsticky_and_reduction.description: The non-sticky regime via the high density lemma, and the reduction of the general case to the sticky case.

WZ.title: Volume estimates for unions of convex sets, and the Kakeya set conjecture
WZ.role: the paper this survey explains; Theorem 1.2 is its main result

WZ1.title: Wang–Zahl, the sticky Kakeya theorem (earlier paper)
WZ1.role: the sticky Kakeya theorem (Thm 6.1) proved in earlier Wang–Zahl work

Bou.title: Bourgain, on the dimension of Kakeya sets and discretized sum-product

BCT.title: Bennett–Carbery–Tao, On the multilinear restriction and Kakeya conjectures
BCT.role: multilinear Kakeya — earlier partial progress on the Kakeya problem; SHARED node with the bourgain-demeter-decoupling DAG (where it is an input to decoupling)
